entry: using the threading and queue libraries, I can have a producer/consumer pattern that does a complete read of the serial data from the feather before proceeding to print them all. If I do it all in a while loop, then I can close out the threads at the end of the loop and reopen the processor thread at the start of the next loop, where the processor thread contains a ser.readline() call that will pause the program again.
Next steps: make a better concurrent serial read so I could load all notifications at once, sort them and dedupe, then print them line by line, rather than receiving notifs one by one.
End to end system v1 is successfully running. I'll increase the polling timeout to 15 minutes to disincentivize checking the display often

Since I don't have unique ids available per notif, I can try creating a unique id through app_id + title + date + sender to dedupe notifications sent by the same sender in the same group. This will be a future improvement.
Background thread is in place, using thread.join() closes out the thread after I've verified we've established connection.
Once the feather is connected to my device, it initially sends an empty object, so I shouldn't start loading notifs right away or the pi will have nothing to read.

Side note: there's a one in a million chance that the feather somehow connects to my iOS device and starts sending data to the pi before the pi has loaded, and we get dropped UART data. Shouldn't be a problem since we're refreshing every so often
I want my 'Connecting . . .' animation to continually play until the first serial data arrives from feather to pi. This will be a concurrency issue solved by a simple background thread.
reminder to tex.encode('ascii').decode('ascii') to get rid of any weird unicode characters
I was worried about the Feather sending payloads faster than the Pi can process, but at 115200 baud with small payloads shouldn't be a problem.
turns out ser.readline() blocks and waits for a newline character, so things stay synchronous. I'll need to have a special END string or something to show the feather is done sending notifs

since I don't have data communication for *removing* notifications, I'll need to poll for new notifications every so often, rather than doing things event-driven. Maybe every 60 seconds I'll just get the fresh batch of unread notifs?

the serial library will take in notifications using ser.readline() until it sees a newline character. Each notification gets stringified using json.dumps on the feather and then does uart.write(string.encode())
Checking permissions on /dev/serial0. it's a symlink to ttyS0, which has a group owner of dialout. I added my user to the group for dialout
error serial.serialutil.PortNotOpenError: Attempting to use a port that is not open
Need to install pyserial in order to properly read UART data. Turns out I have python but not pip installed. Probably breaking system packages given that this pi isn't getting used for anything else.
GND to GND, 3.3v to 3.3v, and TX to RX pins are connected via jumper wire. Hoping nothing gets fried on first try.
Pins have been soldered thanks to my buddy who actuall knows how to solder. I should probably learn, but I'm grateful I didn't reduce my pi and feather pins to blobs.
I'm adding a cron job that will shut my pi off automatically around 9pm. Reminder that my timezone is not necessarily the Pi's timezone

On a feather's gpio, RX pin is 24 and TX is 25. There's a library called 'board' I can import and directly reference these pins as board.TX, making the connection even easier to set up. Soldering is still a couple days away (looking to buy a used kit), while I'm waiting I'll 3d print a couple backup cases
For future self: busio.UART needs a baudrate, the feather can likely handle 115200 as the common default, but I'll make sure pyserial opens my connection at the same rate
Caveat: uart.write() sends data as bytes, so I'll need to run .encode('utf-8') on my string before sending, then decode it on the other end

I was thinking of using pyserial on feather but turns out there's already a built-in busio.UART class that came with the flash installation. I can pass my notifications as a stringified JSON object and send it over UART, with a newline character at the end to signal the end of the stream. I think the pyserial library should interpret this correctly if I just use the readline fn

I'll use the pyserial library to read lines into my rasp pi code. the Pi UART device path is usually /dev/serial0 but I'll want to verify. I'll need to enable serial port hardware in raspi-config before I get any data in
Solder housekeeping: I'll need 1 pin for GND to GND to create a common reference voltage, 1 pin for 3.3v power, and 1pin to feed the feather's TX serial output pin to the pi's RX serial input pin

I was hoping to use more ANSI characters to bold and underline the app names that act as headers for each message section, unfortunately my pi terminal font options are limited and I don't want to try introducing a new font right now.
After fiddling around with f"{test:<10}|" I was able to fill out each sender and message when they are too short for their column width. Using [20:] I can do the proper truncation as well
I'm printing some mock data to the display (success!) but now I want to properly format and truncate the sender/message so that it all is aligned in a table-looking format.

Unfortunately, there's no concept of UUID in this ANCS library. Notifications are sent with a numerical ID counting up from 0 where 0 is the first banked notification. This will make it trickier to overwrite my 'latest message from a sender' if I have the same sender in separate group texts or emails..
As far as data goes, I can get back the app id, title, subtitle (if any, usually group chat name), message, and date sent, in ISO 8601 YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS

Switched back to the circuit python ANCS library in the feather code, it finally showed up for a brief time on my iPad, so I connected. Each subsequent connection on device boot takes anywhere from 30 seconds to 5 minutes, so I'll just keep a timeout running on my pi that will shut down if connection isn't established on my feather after like 20 minutes.
If I set up the feather for UART connection instead of ANCS, it easily connects to the Adafruit ios app for bluetooth connections, so at very least, my device isn't faulty.
Tried using blueutil to scan for available bluetooth devices from my mac, still no luck

When the feather has an open radio connection broadcasting, I should expect it to show up in the iOS setting's list of bluetooth devices, but it's not showing up, even though I've verified the device is pinging.
Added all the necessary circuitPython libraries to my Feather, time to write some ANCS code and establish connection to my iOS device, through which I'll route all notifications
Learned what serial output is, since that's how I read data on my Feather on my mac. I use the `screen` command to get a connection to my peripheral's /dev/tty
Feather has arrived. Flashing the drive and formatting for circuitPython

my terminal doesn't support true RGB color, so I'm getting back very simple coloring on my display. To get a deep amber color, I could try messing with the frame buffer set in my pi's config. Will probably do this later as it's not a priority. Also it's the IBM 3290 that had the amber display, not 3270.
I'd rather use underline text to format but for now I'll just use unicode Box drawing characters to make the edges of the table
reminder to sort notifications in a group based on latest timestamp, but this may not be an issue since notifications will likely be loading in chronologically anyways.

I'll be scrolling through all notifications at a fairly slow rate. If a new notification comes in mid-scroll or gets deleted mid-scroll, I'll let the notification list finish its current scroll before re-fetching the latest state of notifications. This means I'll probably store my notifications on my Feather and send notification data one row at a time to the pi, rather than keep a separate store on the pi.

Because I can't guarantee a unique identifier for messages from the same sender if that sender is posting to a group message, I'll need to use some sort of combination of sender+reciever as a UUID that signals if I should overwrite an existing communication (I only want to show the latest notification from a group text, for example).

I'm thinking a line dedicated to the name of the service (Gmail, Message), then a line with HH:MM | Sender | Message . I'll keep the Sender truncated to a few characters and then fill the rest of the line with a message preview.
Going with bash profile load, disabling systemctl service. Moving on to what my display should be.

If I want to keep auto-login, I can scrap the systemd service and just have my script run in the bashprofile when my tty instance starts up, checking to make sure I'm running it off the main tty and not pty.
Added my user to the tty group, but turns out tty1 has restrictive permissions, possibly due to my auto-login
I added the getty service as an After and Requires to my service under /etc/systemd/system/ but I'm still getting back a permission error after reboot.

systemctl show getty@tty1.service | grep -E 'After|Before|WantedBy' shows me where the terminal load sits in the boot order. My on-boot service should be in the Before section, handled by adding getty to my service's After section

The directive After=multi-user.target may not guarantee that TTY is fully initialized and owned by my user, especially with auto-login configured, which assigns ownership of tty1 to my user. If I make auto-login a pre-req to running my script, that should solve it

systemd service runs on boot but doesn't show up in terminal. It may not be the last thing running and therefore doesn't ever make it to output, or /dev/tty1 isn't set up yet at the moment of run. systemctl status shows a permission denied issue.

'Connecting' text is centered, now to add the dots. I don't want to just be rewriting '.' then '..' then '...', I'd rather have them appear dynamically, so I'll just do a while loop that prints and waits, moving the cursor. I'll then need to clear out just the portion of that line that has the dots in order to reset them.
I learned that ANSI escape codes expect integers, so I need to use Python's // divider instead of /

I can center my text horizontally using print('text'.center(dimensions.columns)), but I need to center it vertically as well. Looks like ANSI escape codes will be the move again. `backslash x1b[10;5H moves to line 10, column 5.`
os.get_terminal_size(tty.fileno()) gives me os.terminal_size(columns=128, lines=37)
I'll use os.get_terminal_size() on my dev/tt1 output to figure out how to properly center my loading screen. I'll animate the classic loading dots

I don't love this approach, but since this doesn't affect the system outside of my script, I'm going to bank sys.stdout and overwrite it with my tty path, then swap back to normal sys.stdout at the end of my script.

First, I'll want to clear out the terminal console. I was thinking just importing subprocess and running subprocess.run(['clear']) but it turns out I can just use ANSI escape codes to clear the screen and mute the flashing cursor
When I ssh into my pi, any script I run only shows up on my ssh'ed terminal. I'll need to reroute my output to my display, something like using echo 'hello world' > /dev/tty1
Something I didn't consider.. I need to solder the pins between the Pi and the Feather.. Only problem is I don't know how to solder

Using systemd, I was able to create a custom service file that runs on boot, so my script will start right off the bat. Corrected file permissions, enabled service, and checked the system logs to ensure it's correctly enabled
using raspi-config I switched to auto-login so I don't have to ssh in with a password every time.
sudo poweroff will be a nice command to shut off the device for the day. Convenient that powering off the pi also powers off the display.

My display wasn't working correctly, had to check I was properly sshing in with nmap and ifconfig. I updated the config.txt on my Pi's OS to ensure it was handling the display correctly. Turns out case for the pi is 3d printed and doesn't seat the HDMI port right, so I'll need to get a different kind of case.

Pi gear arrived today. I flashed a memory card with Pi OS lite. I tried SSHing in with the pi@raspberrypi.local command, but it didn't work. I was able to ssh in with my userid @ the address the Pi takes on my network.
Order for the feather is in. I'm naming this project 'Ripe Banana'
From the spec, Feather typically draws between 300µA and 400µA, which is well within the Pi's power pin limit of 50mA. Aside: I'll need to connect a GND pin between the two devices.
I'm not actually sure if the Feather can transfer data over network, worst case we do Feather TX -> Pi RX pin, and since the Feather runs on 3.3v, I can connect it to the Pi's 3.3v power pin.
ANCS can give me back  app_id, message body, subtitle, and title, which is all I'll need to display

Current plan: iOS -> Feather, format to JSON, send to pi over physical wire connection. Both the Pi and the Feather have UART serial communication pins. I could also run the notifications back and forth over my local network, but we'll keep physical connection as an option

With a bluetooth connection, I can impersonate a BLE peripheral that iOS can forward notifications to. This repo https://github.com/adafruit/Adafruit_CircuitPython_BLE_Apple_Notification_Center is a CircuitPython library so I'll need an add-on that supports it, like the Adafruit Feather nRF52840. There are other libraries that can handle ANCS for raspberry pis directly, but none seem well-supported nor documented.
As an aside, Meta is sunsetting their Instagram / Messenger desktop clients, probably because they're not riddled with enough ads.

/Library/Group Containers/group.com.apple.usernoted is a directory that shows all notifications but this is still on the Mac, not the phone (or ipad?), so it might not have every notification, or it's actually just an extra layer of rerouting from phone to mac to pi.

So with iOS 26.4, Apple is allowing ANCS (their notification system) to forward notifications to third-party devices but ONLY IN THE EU because the EU actually demands this sort of flexibility. So in the future I could just do a direct forwarding to my Pi but for now I'll have to route from iOS device to Pi.

Considered running everything through a Shortcut on iOS with webhooks but the Shortcuts app is limited to only triggers from messages or the Mail app, looks fairly limited overall. Apple must not have enough demand from Shortcut power users. I thought about intercepting iPhone notifications but what if I just forwarded notifications from my device (or another apple device) to my Pi?
Still not loving the way the project is going, it doesn't capture the vision of letting each source of messages be handled independently. Is there an alternative approach?

If I'm hosting chat.db via server on my Mac, should I consider UDP broadcasting or writng to a file that gets rsync'd? Nah, I can just have a localhost server on my network. Also if I want to distinguish messages by service, I might need to have a separate hashmap for each service to store each distinct conversation. Or I could have a hashmap of hashmaps... I should consider which will be most write-efficient.
A lot of these services will give you email notifications if you opt-in, but that heavily loads up Gmail and if the API is unavailable for whatever reason, there go most of my notifications

Drat, a lot of the sources I was hoping to pull messages from don't have a public API. Turns out Meta had quite a few available back in the day but have shuttered anything that's not for businesses.. time to rethink if I'd rather go another route or just condense to the few public APIs I've found (Gmail, Discord) + the chat.db. Gmail can list user messages using https://developers.google.com/workspace/gmail/api/guides/list-messages#code_sample , adding the param q after: XXXXXXX timestamp to get messages for a given day https://developers.google.com/workspace/gmail/api/reference/rest/v1/users.messages/list#query-parameters . I should then consider 'watermarking' a given timestamp each time the pi reboots to make sure I'm only getting messages for a given day.

Thinking more on the APIs, I'll need to be mindful about auth and where I want to store keys. This journal is public, hope no one (no bot) is getting ideas. Side note: I'd probably want to dedupe messages sent by the same sender, so I only read their most recent message.

Spent way too much time fussing over the cheapest but durable hardware. Raspberry Pi Zero 2W + Waveshare 7in display, maybe a push button + dupont jumper wires to have a bootup / shutdown option. Order is in.
I like the idea of a scheduled on/off for the pi. Some sort of cron job, probably. Color scheme for the display will be amber on black (IBM 3270?)
Hardware: doesn't need to be a display bigger than a Nintendo Switch. If I want bigger, I should just get a monitor. I envision a retro display, as though I were running this on an Apple II

The notifications should be categorized by source, with a table header of Source | Time | Sender | first 100 chars of message. If I do the iMessage db route, I'll have to host it on a local server on my laptop. Not optimal if I planned on sleeping my computer.

Raspberry pi seems like the way to go, with an e-ink or mini LED display. Gmail has an accessible API I can fetch from, but I'll need to check that all the other sources I want to pull from do as well. iMessage looks to be a challenge, might be easier to pull from the local chat.db on my computer.

The motive here is that I don't like checking my phone. If I have notifications enabled, they're a nuisance. If I disable notifications, I end up checking the apps anyways. With a mailbox, you look to see if the flag is up, and that tells you if there's mail, but the mailbox isn't yelling at you. Or like bananas. You just look over at the bananas and you have a pretty good idea for whether or not you can eat them.

I want to build a personal notification display, something like a cross between a bloomberg terminal, airport flight monitor, and a hospital bed management display. It should show all my notifications, mail, DMs, texts, and update in realtime.
If I add @latest to the CDN urls I fetch with, I get the latest data
Turns out jsDelivr doesn't do a fresh load of github data every second, but I can purge the CDN references with https://www.jsdelivr.com/tools/purge

I'm moving the journal and goal data over to their own repo and fetching them using cdn.jsdelivr.net so I don't have to keep adding commits to the repo that don't impact the actual code... the things we do to avoid hosting a DB...
Probably could have gone to bed much earlier but I fixed some bugs with how the inverted colors / opacity were getting calculated. Also updated the heatmap color
Spent way too much time figuring out a standard heatmap. Added a 'how i was feeling' section as well.
Success. Only problem is the timestamps. I think they're in GMT rather than local time.
Forgot to hit save on disabling the branch deploy for the daily-goals repo. Triggering redeploy.

I believe I found the issue--on github, there was already a Pages deploy pipeline set up for the daily-goals repository (probably leftover from when it was toe-touching). I wonder if this was interfering with the parent repo's deploy. This happened before with guitar-note-guesser.

Locally everything seems to be working fine when I run a build. So I guess I'll push to prod and see if anything miraculously changes.Just to be safe, I'm adding a <div> wrapper around the daily goals component so that my linter doesn't remove the return statement's parentheses

This did not work due to ' Unable to define routes with duplicate route id: `chord-generator/src/App` 'I suppose rather than pushing code every time to prod to see if the deploy works, I can just run react-router build locally and check the build file for correct index.html files.

Because I removed the daily goal route from the routes.ts file but kept it in the config file, the build on prod failed, Got an error `Unable to prerender path because it does not match any routes: /daily-goals`. So the routing was working but it's routing the wrong file.I'm going to see if adding the chord generator component to the route for daily-goals works

I added daily-set to react-router.config.ts and that got it to behave correctly. So that routing part is working. Daily goals still is showing the default github index.html file, though.I'm going to try removing the route altogether and verify that it removes the route from prod.

For daily goals, I don't have static assets to load, so the basic index.html file I copied over from other files is trying to import non-existent files. The preview response on the page also gets the `you need javascript to run this page` message. Need to figure out why that is, since this works locally without issue.

I tried adding the index.html file back into the src directory and I think the parent app is routing to that file because the page <title> is `React App`. However, I'm get 400s back on the static assets it's trying to load.

Well, I think it's a routing issue due to having index.html and not index.tsx in the root of the daily-goals directory, but I'm not sure, so I'm just going to give daily-goals the same routing setup as other submodules and see what sticks. I probably need to go back and revisit which code is actually used in prod.
Daily goals is showing the old calendar so I wonder if it's something with the index file being left as-is. I moved it to a /public directory to see if this resolves the issue on build.

Here's everything I added:- switched toe-touching goal to be old files - table with month headers - color coding based on criteria (the more I invested in the goal, the more opaque the color) - this also allows for 'inverted' goals where lower values are better (like screen time) - event listener to allow for arrow key navigation between goals I probably won't push goal updates every day, meaning I'll still log my progres locally and upload it any time I feel like it.
Working prototype in place, wow time flies. Nice to have this up and running, though.Will commit and push tomorrow with in-depth description
Tried connecting to Google Sheet via API to fetch daily goal data. No luck. I need Oauth credentials but this is a client-only app. Can't fetch using the public sheet URL either - CORS error.
Calendar isn't routing correctly on prod
Made a good solid start at a test grid that I think will work well.Todo: add in individual goals and a tab/button setup to switch between them

Starting by renaming and repurposing the toe-touching webapp.project url is now daily-goals had to briefly modify files.exclude in vscode to see what .git files needed to be changed (**/.git) turns out its too much of a pain to try modifying the exisiting repo so I'm just deleting it.
Going to make a goal tracker for 2026.

I want to make a `daily chord + key + tempo + time sig` combination, based on day of the year `Math.floor((new Date() - new Date(date.getFullYear(), 0, 0)) / 1000 / 60 / 60 / 24)` would be the way to go
Tomorrow I'll add the same `you've already done this note` section to GuitarNoteFinder

Stumped on figuring out how to properly generate all notes on the fret across all strings. Current issue seems to be the way indices are calculated. It's almost as though these are getting calculated out of order...Oh wait it's because I'm interpreting string 2 (B) as string 5 (A)... no wonder... Dang, I've had it right the whole time.
It's getting late but I want to try whipping up the `find all C3's on a guitar` or maybe just `all C's` to start.

I should do the inverse now--ask the question `where can I find A4 on a guitar?` (A4 being the fourth octave A on a piano)There would be multiple answers for many of these notes, it should be an easy calculation to determine if each note is available on a given string, though. I can tab toggle between finding the note based on string coordinates and vice versa
While I was there I added refs to buttons so I could use arrowkeys as shortcuts to click the buttons. I always forget how useRefs work under the hood, I just remember that they work.

Added grid below to show all the notes I've already covered and ensure that each additional note I reveal is a note I haven't tried yet.I'll add a counter for how  many notes I have remaining and call it a night.

Ok I think the issue was that I had a separate GitHub Pages deploy set up for the guitar repo, but the other submodules don't have that. Deploying this message just to retrigger the run. Will modify the guitar chord repo as well.
For some reason, guitar app did not deploy correctly. It returns the contents of the markdown. Looking into this..

Journal briefly flashes in GMT time before setting locale, can fix this another time. Also I'm going to make a separate repo for the journal entry now that I figured out the routing issues, otherwise the parent branch is going to get cluttered with journal commits.
Going the extra mile and adding better date/time formatting for the journal

Rudimentary guitar tablature in place. Alignment isn't great but this isn't CSS time. Next project would be to create a game where I go through every note on the fretboard. 15 notes * 6 strings = 90 notes.
Before calling it a night, I'm going to add ASCII guitar tablature to the guitar note guesser
Fixing the export worked for remote builds. Deleting all node_modules dirs and removing the build folder seemed to fix things local
The embarrassing bug may be that I forgot to export the App component from App.tsx, let's see...
Turns out I needed to add submodules via `git submodule add <url> <name>` which I forgot I did for the other repos

Forgot to add a .git extension to my new submodule. I thought this would fix the issue on remote builds, but it actually looks like guitar-note-guesser isn't acknowledged as part of the parent repo yet. Need to figure out a sync...
Tried committing all existing files to their corresponding sub-repositories, just to clean things up
Update, restarting the computer did not solve the issue
I'm getting a lovely <i>Error: EMFILE: too many open files, watch<i>when running npm run dev. Not sure what files are open but will probably do a computer restart to kill any lingering processes
This is a pretty rudimentary way to get started. I'll come back one day and make this a proper journal DB.For now it's just a JSON config with hardcoded dates and backtick characters
